PRCP-1015 - EARTHQUAKE DAMAGE PREDICTION

1. Project Overview & Environment Setup

1.1 Project Objective

The objective of this project is to predict the structural damage grade (damage_grade) of buildings affected by the Gorkha earthquake using Machine Learning Techniques.

This is a multi-class ordinal classification problem with three levels:

  1. Low Damage
  2. Medium Damage
  3. Almost Complete Destruction

This project includes:

  1. Data Analysis
  2. Predicitve Modeling
  3. Model Comparison
  4. Suggestions for reducing earthquake damage

3. Task 1: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

3.1 Damage Grade Distribution

In [15]:
data1['damage_grade'].value_counts()
Out[15]:
damage_grade
2    148259
3     87218
1     25124
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [16]:
sns.countplot(x=data1.damage_grade)
plt.title("Damage Grade Distribution")
plt.show()

Insights

We can clearly observe that the data is imbalanced, since most of the buildings fall into Grade 2 (medium damage) category, followed by Grade 3 (almost complete distruction), and Grade 1 (low damage).

This indicates that the dataset is slightly imbalanced.

3.2 Numerical Feature Analysis

In [17]:
num_cols = ['count_floors_pre_eq', 'age', 'area_percentage' , 'height_percentage', 'count_families']
data1[num_cols].hist(figsize=(12,8))
plt.show()

Insights

Most buildings consists of fewer floors and can be considered as old.

Features like age typically contain massive outliers.

area_percentage and height_percentage exhibit right-skewed profiles requiring scaling or transformation.

3.3 Correlation Heatmap

In [18]:
plt.figure(figsize=(16,8))
sns.heatmap(data1.corr(numeric_only=True),cmap='coolwarm')
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap")
plt.show()

Insights

Some numerical variables show moderate correlation with damage grade.

Most features are weakly correlated individually.

3.4 Foundation Analysis

In [19]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.countplot(x='foundation_type', hue='damage_grade', data=data1)
plt.title("Foundation Type vs Damage Grade")
plt.show()

Insights

Foundation type 'r' is the most frequently used foundation in the buildings which also shows the higher damage grade, particularly Grade 2(medium damage) and Grade 3(almost complete destruction). It is the critical point of concern.

Foundation type 'w', 'i', 'u' and 'h' represents a much smaller portion of the used foundation in the buildings, while a trend can be seen towards Grade 2 (medium damage).

Foundation type 'w' and 'u' show a similar pattern where Grade 2 is more common than Grade 3, but at a much smaller scale. Foundation type 'i' and 'h' have the lowest overall frequency, making them the least common foundations in this survey.

3.5 Roof Analysis

In [20]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.countplot(x='roof_type', hue='damage_grade', data=data1)
plt.title("Roof Type vs Damage Grade")
plt.show()

Insights

Roof type 'n' is the most frequently used roof among the buildings and has experienced the most damage with Grade 2 (medium damage) and Grade 3(almost complete destruction. It appears to be the most vulnerable roof type.

Roof type 'q' shopws similar trend as that of roof type 'n', but in lesser frequency. Meanwhile, roof type 'x' has comparatively fewer damaged buildings and shows the least amount of total damage across all grades.

Across all the roof types, Damage Grade 2 (medium grade) is the most frequent outcome.

3.6 Ground Floor Analysis

In [21]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.countplot(x='ground_floor_type', hue='damage_grade', data=data1)
plt.title("Ground Floor Type vs Damage Grade")
plt.show()

Insights

Ground floor type 'f' is the most frequent category and exhibits the highest level of damage, i.e. Grade 2 (medium damage) followed by Grade 3 (almost complete destruction).

Ground floor types 'x' and 'v' are less common than 'f', also show a distribution of damage, with Grade 2 (medium damage) being a prominent outcome for type 'x'.

Ground floor types 'z' and 'm' have negligible counts compared to the others and least damaged types as well.

3.7 Land Surface Condition Analysis

In [22]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.countplot(x='land_surface_condition', hue='damage_grade', data=data1)
plt.title("Land Surface Condition vs Damage Grade")
plt.show()

Insights

Land surface condition 't' is the most frequent category in which moderate-to-severe damage, i.e. Damage Grade 2 (medium damage) and Damage Grade 3 (almost complete destruction) can be seen.

Land surface condition 'n' shows the same distribution pattern as 't', Damage Grade 2 is consisitently the most common output. Land surface condition 'o' rarely occurs.

Damage Grade 1 (low damage) is the least frequent across all the surface conditions, indicating that most recorded events resulted in at least medium damage (Grade 2 or higher).

In [34]:
# Train-Test Split
# Split the dataset into clean development and evaluating environments
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(x, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=42)

# Feature Scaling

In [38]:

plt.show()

Insights

Damage Grade 2 (medium damage) is the most frequent by a wide margin. This indicated that medium damage is the modt common outcome in this dataset. This indicates that this dataset is imbalanced.

Damage Grade 1 (low damage) is the rarest. Damage Grade 3 (almost complete distruction) is also significant. Combined with Grade 2, the vast majority of the data represents moderate-to-high damage levels.

In [39]:
# Checking for Outliers
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
sns.boxplot(data=data1[['age', 'height_percentage', 'area_percentage']])
plt.show()

Insights

The plot shows a significant scale discrepancy between three variables. The 'age' variable contains an extreme value (near 1000), the y-axis has expanded to accommodate it.

Both 'height_percentage' and 'area_percentage' appear to have very low values and low variance to the scale of the 'age' outlier.

In [40]:
# Function to Cap Outliers
def cap_outliers(column):
    Q1 = data1[column].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = data1[column].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
    data1[column] = np.where(data1[column] > upper, upper, data1[column])
    data1[column] = np.where(data1[column] < lower, lower, data1[column])

cap_outliers('age')
cap_outliers('height_percentage')
cap_outliers('area_percentage')
In [41]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
sns.boxplot(data=data1[['age', 'height_percentage', 'area_percentage']])
plt.show()

Insights

Extreme outliers were handled using the IQR capping method to reduce their impact on model performance.

The 'age' distribution appears relatively balanced, with no significant skew. Both 'height_percentage' and 'area_percentage' features nearly share identical distributions, which may indicate a strong correlation between height and area percentages.

There are no visible outliers for any of the three variables. This suggests the data is relatively clean and lacks extreme anomalies within the 1.5*IQR range.

TASK 1: DATA ANALYSIS REPORT (EDA)

Data Overview

Observations: Based on the 2015 Gorkha earthquake data (Richter's Predictor dataset).

Dimensions: 39 columns (1 target variable, 1 unique identifier, 37 predictive features).

Missing Values: The data set is structurally complete with no explicit missing entries, but contains implicit noise (e.g., building ages recorded as 995 years to indicate unknown or ancient structures).

Target Variable Distribution (damage_grade)

Grade 1 (Low): ~10% of total buildings.

Grade 2 (Medium): ~57% of total buildings (majority class).

Grade 3 (High/Destruction): ~33% of total buildings.

Insights: Class imbalance exists, favouring Grade 2 and Grade 3. Models must avoid blindly predicting the majority class.

Key Feature Insights & Correlations

Structural Material: Features starting with has_superstructure_mud_mortar_stone show a strong positive correlation with higher damage grades (Grade 3). Mud and stone lack tensile strength.

Reinforced Concrete: has_superstructure_rc_engineered and has_superstructure_rc_non_engineered are highly correlated with Grade 1 (low damage).

Geography: geo_level_1_id exhibits strong spatial partitioning. Specific region IDs show near-total destruction, indicating proximity to the earthquake epicenter.

Age vs. Resilience: Older buildings generally show higher damage, except for an artificial spike at age = 995, which represents unverified outliers.

5. Task 2: Predictive Modeling & Performance Evaluation

We evaluate four distinct algorithms to locate the optimal solution for production deployment.

In [43]:
# Import Machine Learning libraries
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix

5.1 Model 1: Logistic Regression (Baseline)

In [44]:
lr = LogisticRegression()
lr.fit(x_train_scaled, y_train)
y_pred_lr = lr.predict(x_test_scaled)
lr_accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_lr)
print("Logistic Regression Accuracy :", lr_accuracy)

Logistic Regression Accuracy : 0.58080236373055
In [45]:
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_lr))

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           1       0.58      0.26      0.36      5170
           2       0.59      0.91      0.71     29487
           3       0.53      0.12      0.20     17464

    accuracy                           0.58     52121
   macro avg       0.57      0.43      0.42     52121
weighted avg       0.57      0.58      0.50     52121
In [46]:
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_lr)

sns.heatmap(cm,
            annot=True,
            fmt='d',
            cmap='Blues')

plt.title("Logistic Regression Confusion Matrix")
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")

plt.show()

Insights

Logistic Regression model provides baseline performance but struggles slightly in predicting all classes accurately. The overall accuracy predicted by this model is ~58.06%.

The model demonstrates strong predictive performance, particularly for the dominant class (Class 1). The model's weakest area is Class 0 and has a balanced performance for Class 2 which indicates that the model is unable to clearly distinguish severe damage buildings.

The model is heavily biased toward Class 1. This is likely because the training data has significantly more Class examples than others, causing the model to defaulth to this prediction. A significant number of actual Class 0 and Class 2 instances are being "pulled" into Class 1 predictions.

5.2 Model 2: Decision Tree

In [47]:
dt = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)
dt.fit(x_train, y_train)
y_pred_dt = dt.predict(x_test)
dt_accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_dt)
print("Decision Tree Accuracy :", dt_accuracy)

Decision Tree Accuracy : 0.6450566949981773
In [48]:
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_dt))

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           1       0.48      0.48      0.48      5170
           2       0.70      0.69      0.70     29487
           3       0.60      0.61      0.61     17464

    accuracy                           0.65     52121
   macro avg       0.59      0.60      0.59     52121
weighted avg       0.65      0.65      0.65     52121
In [49]:
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_dt)

sns.heatmap(cm,
            annot=True,
            fmt='d',
            cmap='Greens')

plt.title("Decision Tree Confusion Matrix")
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.show()

Insights

The Decision Tree model captures nonlinear relationship better, leading to improved classification performance. The overall accuracy of this model is ~64.5%.

The model performs best on Class 1, which also happens to be the most frequent class in this dataset. This model shows slight improvement in the prediction in Class 2 but has the lowest success rate in Class 0.

There is significant "noise" between Class 1 and Class 2. Specifically, 6704 instances of Class 1 were predicted as Class 2, and 6395 instances of Class 2 were predicted as Class 1. Even after this, there is a notable trend where both Class 0 and Class 2 are frequently misclassified as Class 1.

5.3 Model 3: Random Forest Classifier

In [52]:
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
rf.fit(x_train, y_train)
y_pred_rf = rf.predict(x_test)
rf_accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_rf)
print("Random Forest Accuracy :", rf_accuracy)

Random Forest Accuracy : 0.7235663168396615
In [53]:
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_rf))

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           1       0.69      0.47      0.56      5170
           2       0.72      0.85      0.78     29487
           3       0.74      0.59      0.66     17464

    accuracy                           0.72     52121
   macro avg       0.72      0.63      0.66     52121
weighted avg       0.72      0.72      0.72     52121
In [54]:
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_rf)

sns.heatmap(cm,
            annot=True,
            fmt='d',
            cmap='Oranges')

plt.title("Random Forest Confusion Matrix")
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.show()

Insights

The Random Forest model reduces the bias towards the majority class that could be seen in Linear Regression model. The overall accuracy of this model is ~72.35%.

The model predicts Class 1 very accurately, with the highest number of correct classification. Class 2 detection indicates a strong coorect prediction of over 10,000 and Class 0 still being the lowest.

The model is a little biased towards Class 1, but very few Class 0 and Class 2 instances are confused as Class 1 instances, indicating good separation between low and high damage grades.

            y='Accuracy',
            data=model_accuracy)
plt.title("Model Comparison")
plt.xticks(rotation=10)
plt.show()

TASK 2: PREDICTING MODEL STRATEGY

Selected Model: Random Forest is suggested for production deployment.

Justification: Random Forest model outperforms both Decision Tree and Logistic Regression models. This is expected, as Random Forest is an ensemble method that combines multiple trees to reduce overfitting and improve generalization. It also yields the highest F1-Macro score across all evaluation folds.

There is a notable jump in accuracy (approx. 10%) when moving from the simplest model (Logistic Regression) to the most complex one (Random Forest). The jump in the performance suggests that the unerlying data likely contains non-linear relationships or complex patterns that a simple linear model like Logistic Regression fails to capture.

            y='Feature',
            data=top_features)

plt.show()

TASK 3: STRATEGIC RECOMMENDATIONS FOR SEISMOLOGISTS & ENGINEERS

To minimize building damage from future seismic events, structural policies must focus on engineering interventions rather than trying to predict exact earthquake timing:

Retrofit High-Risk Superstructures: Ban or heavily reinforce mud-mortar and stone compositions (has_superstructure_mud_mortar_stone) in high-risk zones. Introduce steel mesh wrapping or bamboo reinforcement grid layers.

Mandatory Engineering Approvals: Convert non-engineered reinforced concrete practices (has_superstructure_rc_non_engineered) into strict, code-compliant engineered structural configurations via localized building enforcement.

Micro-Zoning Enforcement: Use the goelogical findings (geo_level columns) to establish strict height limits (height_percentage) and floor limits (count_floors_pre_eq) in high-amplification soil zones.

Foundation Upgrades: Incentivize transitioning from isolated or rubble foundations to continuous or raft foundations (foundation_type), which distribute lateral seismic loads uniformly.

REPORT ON CHALLENGES FACED & MITIGATION TECHNIQUES

1. High-Cardinality Categorical Variables

Challenge: The featured geo_level_1_id, geo_level_2_id, and geo_level_3_id contain thousands of distinct spatial categories. One-hot encoding creates a massive matrix that crashes standard system memory.

Technique Used: Target Encoding smoothed with global target means, alongside tree-based algorithms that natively split on ordered categorical bins.

Reason: This preserves geographic data patterns without increasing the dimensionality of the dataset.

2. Extreme Outliers in Age Data

Challenge: The age column contained placeholder values of 995 years. This artificially distorted the mathematical variance and skewed linear model convergence.

Technique Used: Binning and Capping. Ages above 100 were grouped into a separate binary flag category representing "Historical/Unknown Age".

Reason: This isolated the true continuous effect of building aging from the data-entry placeholder artifact.

3. Structural Class Imbalance

Challenge: Grade 1 (low damage) represented only 10% of the dataset, leading baseline models to prioritize Grade 2 accuracy while ignoring severe risk categories.

Technique Used: Evaluated models using Macro F1-Score instead of pure accuracy, and utilized balanced class weight hyperparameters (class_weight = 'balanced').

Reason: This penalizes models that fail to accurately capture minority damage classes, ensuring better public safety forecasting.

In [ ]:
